resume:
Priya Nair
Customer Support Specialist (Tier 1 / Tier 2) — SaaS and Fintech
Email: priya.nair.support@example.com | Location: Remote (UTC+5:30) | Open to UK/EU shifts

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
I am an early-career customer support professional with just under three years of hands-on experience supporting software-as-a-service and consumer-fintech products over chat, email, and voice channels. I started on a Tier 1 queue answering high-volume billing and login questions, and over time I was trusted with Tier 2 escalations involving payment reconciliation, API-key troubleshooting for small-business integrators, and edge cases where a customer's data did not match what our internal tooling showed. I care a great deal about getting the facts right before I respond, because in fintech a wrong answer about a refund or a settlement timeline is not just annoying for the customer, it can create a compliance problem for the company. I am comfortable reading logs, reproducing bugs in a sandbox, and writing a clean handoff ticket to engineering when something is genuinely broken rather than just bouncing the customer around. I am looking for a Tier 2 role where I can keep deepening my technical troubleshooting and start mentoring newer agents on the queue.

EXPERIENCE

BrightLedger (consumer budgeting and bill-pay app) — Customer Support Specialist
March 2023 to present (remote)
BrightLedger is a mobile-first personal-finance app with roughly four hundred thousand monthly active users. I joined as the fifth person on the support team and I now sit on a team of eleven. My core responsibilities:
- I handle a daily mix of around forty to fifty contacts across in-app chat and email, with a personal first-response-time target of under fifteen minutes during my shift and a same-day resolution target for anything that is not a confirmed bug.
- I own the payments escalation lane, which means when a customer says a bill-pay did not arrive at the biller, I am the one who traces the payment through our ledger, checks the processor status webhook, and determines whether the money is in transit, returned, or genuinely stuck. I close roughly thirty of these payment-trace tickets a week.
- I write and maintain twelve macros in our help-desk tool covering the most common billing, refund-timeline, and account-recovery questions, and I review them every quarter against the latest policy so agents are never quoting a settlement window that changed.
- I run a weekly thirty-minute office hours for the three newest agents where we walk through one real (anonymised) hard ticket and I show them how I read the payment ledger and the processor logs.
Selected results I can speak to in detail:
- In my first eight months I helped bring the team median time-to-resolution down from about twenty-six hours to about nine hours, mostly by building the payments-trace runbook so agents stopped escalating every payment question to engineering. I did not do this alone, but I wrote the runbook and ran the training.
- My personal CSAT over the last two quarters has been ninety-four percent on a five-point survey with about six hundred responses, against a team average of eighty-eight percent.
- I personally caught a recurring defect where a specific biller's confirmation webhook was being parsed a day late, which was generating a wave of false 'payment failed' tickets; I documented the reproduction steps and the engineering fix dropped our payment-related contact volume by roughly eighteen percent the following month.

Northwind Mobile (prepaid telecom MVNO) — Tier 1 Support Agent
June 2021 to February 2023 (hybrid, then remote)
This was my first full-time support role. Northwind sold prepaid mobile plans to about ninety thousand subscribers. I worked a phone-and-chat queue with strict average-handle-time expectations.
- I handled around seventy contacts a day, mostly top-ups, plan changes, SIM activation, and coverage complaints, holding an average handle time of about six minutes against a team target of seven.
- I was promoted after eleven months to be a queue 'floater', meaning I took the calls that newer agents could not resolve and decided whether they needed a supervisor.
- I built the first version of an internal cheat-sheet for SIM-activation failures that reduced repeat contacts on that issue noticeably; my supervisor later turned it into the official onboarding doc.
- I learned the discipline of not promising a customer something I could not verify, after early on I told a customer their number would port in two hours and it took two days, which taught me to quote the documented worst-case window instead of the best case.

EARLIER
Campus IT Help Desk (university, part-time) — Student Support Assistant
September 2019 to May 2021
- Reset accounts, troubleshot Wi-Fi and printing, logged tickets, and covered the walk-up desk during exam weeks when volume tripled.

SKILLS AND TOOLS
Help-desk platforms: Zendesk, Intercom, Freshdesk. Comfortable building macros, triggers, and simple views.
Technical troubleshooting: reading application and webhook logs, reproducing issues in a staging sandbox, basic SQL SELECT queries to confirm account state, Postman for checking API responses on integrator tickets.
Payments and fintech literacy: understand the difference between authorisation, capture, settlement, and refund timelines; familiar with ACH return codes at a practical level; careful about never disclosing account details without identity verification.
Communication: clear written English, calm phone manner, experience de-escalating frustrated customers, writing structured engineering handoffs with reproduction steps.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
A Tier 2 customer support role at a fintech or SaaS company where accuracy matters, where I can keep growing my log-reading and troubleshooting depth, and where there is a path toward a senior-agent or team-lead track within a couple of years. I work well asynchronously across time zones and I am happy to cover UK or European hours.
jd: BrightLedger's larger competitor, Finch Pay, is hiring a Tier 2 Customer Support Specialist for its bill-pay and money-movement product. Reporting to the Support Team Lead, you will be the escalation point for payment-trace and reconciliation issues that Tier 1 cannot resolve: tracing a transaction across the ledger and processor, determining whether funds are in transit, returned, or stuck, and writing precise engineering handoffs when there is a genuine defect. Accuracy is non-negotiable — quoting a customer the wrong settlement window or refund timeline can create a regulatory issue, so we want someone who verifies before they answer. You will also maintain macros and the team's troubleshooting runbook and help coach newer agents. We are looking for one to three years of support experience, ideally in fintech or a regulated SaaS product, comfort reading logs and running basic SQL, and a demonstrated habit of quantifying their own impact (CSAT, time-to-resolution, contact deflection). This is a junior-to-mid individual-contributor role with a clear path to senior agent.

history.q: Tell me about your current role at BrightLedger and what you own day to day.
You mention you own the payments escalation lane. Walk me through exactly what you do when a customer says a bill-pay didn't arrive.
How many of those payment-trace tickets do you close in a typical week?
You said you helped bring team time-to-resolution from 26 hours to 9. What specifically did you build, and what did the rest of the team do?
Tell me about the webhook defect you caught. How did you figure out it was a parsing delay and not something on the customer's side?
What was the measurable impact of that fix?
Give me an example of a time you didn't know the answer and had to tell the customer that.
Your resume mentions you quote the 'documented worst-case window' instead of the best case. Why does that matter in fintech specifically?
Last one: walk me through how you'd hand off a genuine defect to engineering so they can actually act on it.

candidate_last_answer: I write the ticket so an engineer can reproduce it without messaging me. Three parts. First, exact reproduction steps: account ID, the bill-pay transaction ID, the timestamp in UTC, and which biller. Second, what I observed versus what should have happened — for the webhook bug, I attached the processor's confirmation timestamp and our ledger's status-update timestamp and showed the gap was about twenty-six hours, consistently, across four accounts I'd collected. Third, blast radius and a guess at the cause: I noted it was only that one biller's webhook, that it affected roughly two hundred contacts that week, and that my hypothesis was a late parse on our side, not a customer error. I tag it P2 with the affected-account list attached, and I link the customer tickets so support can close them in a batch once it's fixed. I don't guess at the fix in code, but I give them everything to start in minute one.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: my hypothesis was a late parse on our side, not a customer error

expected-yield: how the candidate ruled out a customer-side or processor-side cause before asserting a late parse on their own system — the specific evidence or test that distinguished an internal parsing delay from the alternatives, confirming the diagnosis was reasoned rather than assumed
